quote: No. The thing is the person said that, you guys have a Delta solar panel for me to take a look at. But I was saying I already have solar panel. All I needed was, like, batteries.
stewart_read: The customer is flagging two things simultaneously: (1) a product-name artifact from the prior touch ('Delta solar panel') that doesn't match Ion's catalog, and (2) their actual need — battery only, existing solar system already in place. This is the orphaned-flow trigger. Carter's response ('Just a battery? Okay') acknowledges the need but skips the entire qualification sequence: own vs. lease, original installer, system size, working status. On a recycled lead, the prior touch may have captured some of this — but Carter doesn't reference any prior data, and the closer will have no qualification context. Strategic read: Carter may be deferring qualification to the closer given this is a rebook. Reactive read: Carter simply didn't recognize the orphaned-flow trigger. The reactive read is more supported — there's no evidence of deliberate deferral, and the 'Just a battery? Okay' response reads as acceptance, not strategy.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter recognized this as a recycled lead where qualification data may already be on file and deliberately deferred orphaned-flow to the closer.
coaching_implication: Walk Carter through the orphaned-flow checklist (schema section 7) and explain why it applies on rebooks, not just first touches. The closer needs to know: own vs. lease, installer, system size, working status. If prior-touch data covers this, Carter should reference it — 'I see we have your system info on file' — rather than leaving a gap.

quote: you guys have a Delta solar panel for me to take a look at
stewart_read: 'Delta solar panel' is an unresolved product-name artifact from the prior touch. It doesn't match Ion's catalog (Ion sells its own panels, not a 'Delta' brand). Carter does not clarify what this refers to, does not correct the customer's framing, and does not establish what Ion actually offers. The customer may arrive at the closer appointment expecting a product Ion doesn't carry. This is a narrower version of the tesla_expectation_gap pattern — not Tesla roof tiles, but a named product from a prior rep's framing that needs to be resolved before the closer call.
alternative_hypothesis: 'Delta solar panel' may be a customer mishearing or misremembering the prior rep's language, and Carter may have correctly judged it as not worth correcting on a rebook call.
coaching_implication: Carter should have asked one clarifying question: 'When they mentioned Delta solar panels, do you remember what they said about it?' This surfaces whether the prior rep made a product promise Ion can't fulfill, and gives the closer a clean handoff. Flag for the closer: customer arrived with a product name that doesn't match Ion's catalog — clarify at the top of the appointment.

quote: Yeah. We can reschedule that for you. I think let me look at yeah. It says it's just for a battery. That's just a generic text that goes out.
stewart_read: Carter's transparency about the generic text ('That's just a generic text that goes out') is a small trust-building move — it explains the product mismatch the customer flagged without being defensive. This is a micro-trade: Carter substitutes a process explanation for a personal commitment, which reduces the customer's mild corrective friction. The move works — the customer doesn't push back further on the mismatch. However, it's a floor-level trust move, not a ceiling-level one. Carter could have followed it with a direct acknowledgment of the customer's actual need to deepen the trust signal.
alternative_hypothesis: The generic-text explanation could read as deflection — 'it's not my fault, it's the system.' If the customer were more skeptical, this framing might increase rather than reduce friction.
coaching_implication: The transparency move was correct. Coach Carter to follow it with a direct bridge: 'So what I have for you is specifically about the battery — that's what we're going to focus on.' This closes the loop the generic-text explanation opens.

quote: Okay. What about gonna be in July next week. I'll be back on Tuesday.
stewart_read: The customer is actively problem-solving the scheduling — they're not looking for an exit, they're looking for a time that works. 'I'll be back on Tuesday' is a buying signal: this customer wants the appointment. Carter correctly reads this and pivots to Tuesday, then Wednesday when Tuesday proves difficult. The signal is handled adequately. What Carter misses is the opportunity to anchor the customer's motivation at this moment — the customer is clearly invested enough to work around a tight schedule, which is the emotional hook for the closer. Carter never asks why the battery matters to them, which would have given the closer a value anchor.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may have correctly judged that probing motivation on a rebook call would feel intrusive or slow down a customer who just wants to confirm a time.
coaching_implication: The scheduling cooperation is a green light to ask one curiosity question: 'Sounds like the battery is important to you — is it mainly for backup power, or something else?' This takes 10 seconds and gives the closer a motivation anchor. Coach Carter to recognize scheduling cooperation as an invitation to deepen, not just confirm.

quote: K. Sounds good. K. We'll do 11AM next Saturday. That's gonna be the twenty fifth.
stewart_read: Carter books the appointment but delivers none of the required button-up elements: no one-hour block stated, no at-computer requirement, no 'is that fair?' verbal handshake, no phone-appointment specification. The only protocol element present is the text-confirmation promise ('I'll shoot you a text'). Per the schema, skipping the at-computer and no-driving anchors increases no-show rate. On a Saturday appointment booked a week out, the no-show risk is already elevated — the missing button-up elements compound it.
alternative_hypothesis: On a rebook call where the customer is already familiar with Ion's process, Carter may have judged that re-running the full button-up would feel redundant or patronizing.
coaching_implication: The strategic read has some merit on a rebook, but the 'is that fair?' handshake and at-computer requirement are low-friction and high-value regardless of prior familiarity. Walk Carter through the button-up checklist and identify which elements are non-negotiable even on rebooks. The verbal handshake in particular is a 3-second move that materially affects show rate.

quote: I'll shoot you a text so you have it in writing. Okay?
stewart_read: Carter promises a text confirmation, which is the right move. However, per the schema, the text must include a tieback to something specific the customer said — not just appointment logistics. Carter has no material to tieback with because no curiosity questions were asked about why the battery matters. The text will almost certainly be generic ('I have you on the calendar for Saturday the 25th at 11AM') with no personalization. A generic text is better than no text, but it misses the show-rate reinforcement the tieback is designed to provide.
coaching_implication: Show Carter the text_after_call template and the tieback requirement. On this specific call, the only available tieback is the battery need — 'looking forward to going over the battery options with you.' That's thin but better than nothing. The deeper fix is upstream: ask one curiosity question during the call so the text has something real to anchor on.

stewart_read: Carter's response to the battery-only ask is acceptance without any qualification or context-setting. Ion carries exactly one battery product: the Tesla Powerwall. Carter does not confirm this is what the customer needs, does not ask about the customer's existing system compatibility, and does not set any expectation about what the closer will be presenting. The closer will arrive at this appointment without knowing whether the customer's existing system is Powerwall-compatible, what installer did the original work, or whether the customer has a realistic expectation of what Ion can offer. This is a knowledge gap in the handoff, not necessarily in Carter's product knowledge.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may have confirmed battery-specific details in the prior touch that are on file, making re-qualification redundant.
coaching_implication: Coach Carter to ask one qualifying question when a battery-only need is stated: 'Do you know who installed your current system?' This surfaces compatibility context for the closer and takes 10 seconds. If prior-touch data covers it, Carter should reference it explicitly rather than leaving a gap.

quote: Yeah. Because, I mean, the the thing is we start at 10:00 your time and go till 07:00 your time.
stewart_read: Carter correctly identifies the operating window constraint and communicates it clearly to the customer. This is competent scheduling navigation. However, the framing ('we start at 10:00 your time and go till 07:00 your time') is purely logistical — it doesn't anchor the appointment as valuable or create any anticipation. The customer's response ('So that's gonna be when you're working') is resigned acceptance, not enthusiasm. Carter had an opportunity here to briefly reframe the Saturday slot as a positive ('Saturday morning is actually a great time — you'll have the whole day ahead of you') but doesn't take it.
coaching_implication: Minor coaching point — the scheduling navigation was correct. The opportunity is to add one sentence of positive framing when landing on a constrained slot, so the customer arrives at Saturday with mild anticipation rather than mild resignation.
